title.value: Barriers and enabling factors for error analysis in NLG research
title.evidence: Barriers and enabling factors for error analysis in NLG research

year.evidence: This position paper takes the stance that error analyses should be encouraged, and discusses several ways to do so.
year.notes: Year of publication inferred from the context of the paper.

abstract.value: Earlier research has shown that few studies in Natural Language Generation (NLG) evaluate their system outputs using an error analysis, despite known limitations of automatic evaluation metrics and human ratings. This position paper takes the stance that error analyses should be encouraged, and discusses several ways to do so. This paper is based on our shared experience as authors as well as a survey we distributed as a means of public consultation. We provide an overview of existing barriers to carrying out error analyses, and propose changes to improve error reporting in the NLG literature.
abstract.evidence: Earlier research has shown that few studies in Natural Language Generation (NLG) evaluate their system outputs using an error analysis, despite known limitations of automatic evaluation metrics and human ratings. This position paper takes the stance that error analyses should be encouraged, and discusses several ways to do so. This paper is based on our shared experience as authors as well as a survey we distributed as a means of public consultation. We provide an overview of existing barriers to carrying out error analyses, and propose changes to improve error reporting in the NLG literature.

doi.notes: No DOI provided in the paper.

venue.notes: No specific venue mentioned in the paper.

background.value: Error analysis is a formalised procedure through which researchers identify and categorise errors in system output. In the context of Natural Language Generation (NLG), error identification often means manually annotating the output text, ideally with multiple annotators.
background.evidence: Error analysis is a formalised procedure through which researchers identify and categorise errors in system output. In the context of Natural Language Generation (NLG), error identification often means manually annotating the output text, ideally with multiple annotators (van Miltenburg et al., 2021a).

problem_statement.value: Despite the usefulness of error analyses, relatively few NLG papers included them (about 11% of the papers surveyed). It is unclear why most authors do not report error analyses in their work, or how we might encourage authors to carry out an error analysis.
problem_statement.evidence: Despite the usefulness of error analyses, Van Miltenburg et al. (2021a) have shown in their survey of INLG papers published in 2010, 2015, and 2020 that relatively few NLG papers included them (about 11% of the papers surveyed). Gehrmann et al. (2022) provide a similarly low number (about 23% of papers published at ACL, INLG, or EMNLP 2021). It is unclear why most authors do not report error analyses in their work, or how we might encourage authors to carry out an error analysis.

value: Researchers and practitioners interested in NLG research
Participants included academics and industry professionals.
participants.evidence: Our survey targets researchers and practitioners interested in NLG research. To maximize our reach, we spread our survey through Discord, Slack, Twitter, and the Corpora and SIGGEN mailing lists.

value: Experience reading error analyses
Experience carrying out error analyses
Barriers and enabling factors to carry out error analyses
Necessity and usefulness of error analyses
Reporting practices
Other comments
questions.evidence: Participants were asked some general demographic questions, followed by questions about the following topics: Experience reading error analyses, Experience carrying out error analyses, Barriers and enabling factors to carry out error analyses, Necessity and usefulness of error analyses, Reporting practices, Other comments.

data_analysis.value: We performed a quantitative analysis of the responses to our closed questions. In addition, we performed a qualitative analysis of the open question responses, inspired by thematic analysis and grounded theory.
data_analysis.evidence: We performed a quantitative analysis of the responses to our closed questions. In addition, we performed a qualitative analysis of the open question responses, inspired by other qualitative approaches, such as thematic analysis (Braun and Clarke, 2006) and grounded theory (e.g., Strauss and Corbin 1994).

value: 33 out of 49 participants found reading an error analysis at least moderately useful.
Only about 11% of surveyed NLG papers included error analyses.
The majority of participants agreed that error analyses should be included in both journal and conference papers.
quantitative_results.evidence: Of the 49 participants that answered this part of the survey, the majority (33) recalled having read an error analysis in an NLG paper. Most respondents found reading an error analysis at least moderately useful, and no respondent found it not useful. ... Almost all of our participants agreed that journal submissions should include an error analysis, and the majority of our respondents also agreed that the same should hold for conference papers.

value: Participants highlighted that error analysis helps identify remaining challenges and direct future work.
Some respondents indicated that thorough error analyses are better than cherry-picked examples more commonly seen in qualitative analyses.
Error analyses were seen as a good complement to imperfect metrics.
qualitative_results.evidence: By far the most common answer was that error analysis could help identify remaining challenges and direct future work, both at a high level, and in terms of improving individual systems. Several responses also mentioned researchers’ bias and noted that a thorough error analysis is better than cherry-picked examples more commonly seen in a qualitative analysis section.

value: Time constraints were identified as a primary barrier to conducting error analyses.
Lack of resources, including funding and tools for error analysis.
Cultural factors in the research community that do not reward or require error analyses.
barriers.evidence: Most participants thought a culture change is necessary to make error analysis a common practice. ... Time and money were considered by our respondents to be the main barriers to carrying out error analyses.

value: Increased recognition and rewards for error analyses in publication processes.
Availability of dedicated tools and resources for conducting error analyses.
Greater collaboration among researchers to share the workload of conducting error analyses.
enabling_factors.evidence: Participants proposed making error analysis a requirement for papers and explicitly recognising it in review forms; this should highlight its importance both for research and industrial/commercial applications.

conclusion.value: By removing or minimizing barriers and motivating authors to include error analyses in their work, we may see greater adoption of error analysis in the future. Future work should focus on developing tools and resources to encourage more routine and robust error analyses.
conclusion.evidence: In the future, we would like to focus on developing tools and resources, such as error taxonomies, annotation tools, and clear guidelines that would help to encourage more routine and robust error analyses.

irb_approval.evidence: Before carrying out our study, we obtained ethical approval from the Institutional Review Board (IRB) at the lead author’s university.

participant_privacy.value: Participants were informed about their rights, and measures were taken to ensure anonymity and confidentiality.
participant_privacy.evidence: Our study starts with an information letter, describing the goal of the study, the expected duration, and potential risks/benefits of the study. ... Our considerations for the IRB are detailed below.
